column1: # error_sites.tsv — every error-throwing call site → corresponding Err(...)

column3: notes

column1: # STATUS: populated. Patterns grouped by C-side error function. One row per
# distinct call shape; specific verbatim call sites in the source tree are
# instances of these patterns. The Rust equivalent is the canonical form
# to emit at the call site (see PORTING.md §4.2 and §6).
#  - `state` is the conventional binding for `&mut LuaState`.
#  - `?` propagates from any helper that itself returns `Result<_, LuaError>`.
#      LuaError::type_error(v, op)     -> Runtime with "attempt to <op> a <t> value"
#      LuaError::concat_error(p1, p2)  -> Runtime, typeerror on first non-coercible
#      LuaError::arith_error(p1, p2)   -> Runtime, "attempt to perform arithmetic"
#      LuaError::int_overflow(p1, p2)  -> Runtime, "number ... has no integer representation"
#      LuaError::call_error(v)         -> Runtime, "attempt to call a <t> value"
#      LuaError::with_status(status)   -> variant chosen by status code

column3: generic runtime error; in C also formats msg, prepends source:line for Lua frames, then routes through luaG_errormsg → luaD_throw. The Rust constructor records the call site's source/line via `state.current_loc()` if available.
attempt to <op> a <type> value [extra]. `extra` is `varinfo` (local/upvalue/global name) — supply via `state.varinfo(o)` inside the constructor.
attempt to call a <type> value. Variant of type_error with op="call" and an extended varinfo via funcnamefromcall.
bad 'for' <what> (number expected, got <type>).
attempt to concatenate a <type> value. In C, dispatches to typeerror on whichever of p1/p2 isn't a string/number.
Used for bit ops where a float operand has no int representation. Routes to typeerror on the offending operand.
number%s has no integer representation. The `%s` is `varinfo`; pass the offending operand to the constructor.
attempt to compare two <t> values or "attempt to compare <t1> with <t2>".

Raises the value already on top of stack. In C, also invokes the message handler if `errfunc != 0` then luaD_throw(LUA_ERRRUN). In Rust the message-handler step happens inside `state.protected_call`; here we just propagate.
NOT an error per se — the line/count hook may *itself* raise, in which case `?` propagates. Keep as a fallible call, not an Err site.

Final throw point in C (setjmp/longjmp). In Rust, every caller already builds the Err — this row exists for completeness when porting code that calls luaD_throw directly with a precomputed status code (LUA_ERRMEM, LUA_ERRRUN, LUA_ERRSYNTAX, LUA_ERRERR).
CATCHER, not thrower. Returns `Result<(), LuaError>`; caller inspects the Err variant to recover the status. Replaces the setjmp landing pad.
CATCHER. Higher-level than rawrunprotected: also resets stack and CallInfo on error. Returns `Result<(), LuaError>`.
memory allocation error: block too big. In C this throws via luaD_throw(LUA_ERRMEM).
Macro: `luaD_throw(L, LUA_ERRMEM)`. Use directly when an allocator returns NULL.
The Rust allocator returns `Result<*mut T, LuaError>`; `?` propagates `LuaError::Memory`. No explicit `luaM_error` call site needed at the use point.
Macro: tests `n * e` overflow then calls `luaM_toobig`. Folded into the safe allocator wrapper.

column1: # --- string-building (lobject.c) — NOT throwers; usually paired with a throw ---

column3: Builds a Lua string on top of the stack and returns its C pointer. The `?` propagates only OOM. In C this is frequently the immediate argument of `luaG_runerror`/`luaX_syntaxerror`; in Rust collapse the two-step pattern into a single `Err(LuaError::runtime(format_args!(...)))` whenever possible.
va_list form; almost never used outside `luaG_runerror`'s internals. In Rust, prefer the `format_args!` constructor on `LuaError::runtime` and skip the push-then-throw indirection.
C-API top-level error; equivalent to `luaL_where + pushvfstring + lua_concat + lua_error`. In Rust, the constructor prepends location info from the current CallInfo.
bad argument #N to '<fname>' (extramsg). `<fname>` is derived from the calling C function name; in Rust the constructor reads it from the current CallInfo / hook context.
<tname> expected, got <actual type>. Wraps `luaL_argerror` in C.
Macro: `cond || luaL_typeerror(...)`. Translate to an explicit guard.
Macro form of argerror.
Throws "stack overflow (msg)" if growth fails. Returns `Result<(), LuaError>`.
Throws type_arg_error on mismatch. Constants like LUA_TTABLE map to `LuaType::Table`.
Throws `LuaError::arg_error(narg, "value expected")` if narg is none/nil-absent.
Throws type_arg_error("string") if non-string and non-coercible-number.
Macro for `luaL_checklstring(L, n, NULL)`.
Throws type_arg_error("number").
Throws either "number expected, got <t>" or `LuaError::arg_error(narg, "number has no integer representation")`.
Throws type_arg_error("tname"). Verifies metatable identity.
Throws `LuaError::arg_error(narg, "invalid option '<s>'")`.

Parser/lexer error at current token. In C this prepends `<source>:<line>:` and appends ` near <token>` (via lexerror), then luaD_throw(LUA_ERRSYNTAX). The Rust constructor reads source+line from `LexState`.
Like syntaxerror but for a specific token (not always the current one); used in numeric/string literal scanning. Internal in C (static `lexerror`); only `syntaxerror` is exported.
Static helper in lparser.c. In Rust make it a free fn in the parser module returning `Result<Never, LuaError>` or just inline.

column2: return Err(LuaError::syntax(format_args!("too many {} (limit is {}) in {}", what, limit, where_str)))
column3: Static helper for compile-time-limit overruns (locals, upvalues, constants, etc.).
Helper that raises a syntax error mentioning the opening token if the closing token is missing on a different line. Fallible — propagate with `?`.
Macro form of `if (!cond) luaX_syntaxerror(ls, msg);`.
Pops the error value from the stack and raises it. Special-cases the global "not enough memory" string to raise `LuaError::Memory` instead — replicate that check in `from_value`.
CATCHER (alias for luaD_pcall at the C API layer). Returns `Result<(), LuaError>`; the status code is reconstructed by the caller from the Err variant if needed.
NOT an error site. The panic handler runs when an Err escapes a thread with no protected frame; in Rust this typically becomes a `process::abort` analogue in `lua-cli`.
